overview.purpose: Soupify is a file concatenation format that bundles multiple source files into a single markdown file for transmission to AI systems. Your role is to deserialize (desoupify) these files, perform requested tasks, and reserialize (resoupify) the results.
workflow: Receive a soupified (.soup) file containing multiple source files
Parse the soup block headers to reconstruct the original files
Create the original files in your working environment
Perform the requested tasks (analysis, modifications, debugging, etc.)
Resoupify the modified files back into a single soup file
Return the soup file so the user can desoupify to get edited files

soup_format_specification.description: A soup file is a markdown file containing concatenated source files, each preceded by a soup block header.

soup_marker.description: Required marker identifying the start of a soup block

file_path.description: The original file's path, double-quoted and properly escaped
notes: Path separators use forward slashes (/) even on Windows
Special characters in paths are escaped as needed
Relative or absolute paths should be preserved as provided

souped_lines.description: The exact number of lines in the file content that follows

trailing_newline.description: Boolean indicating whether the original file ended with a newline character
notes: This is crucial for exact file restoration
Each file's content follows immediately after its soup block header
Content spans exactly the number of lines specified in #SOUPED_LINES
Multiple soup blocks are concatenated sequentially in the soup file
No extra separators between soup blocks—next header follows immediately

desoupify.description: Convert a soup file back into the original files
algorithm: 1. Open and read the entire soup file
2. Scan for lines matching the pattern: #SOUP "<path>" #SOUPED_LINES <N> #SOUP_TRAILING_NEWLINE <bool>
a. Extract the file path from the quoted string
b. Extract the line count (N) to know how many lines follow
c. Extract the trailing newline boolean
d. Read exactly N lines after the header into a buffer
e. Create parent directories for the file path as needed
f. Write the buffer to disk at the specified path
g. If trailing_newline is false, remove the final newline from the file
4. Repeat until all soup blocks are processed
implementation_notes: Handle path escaping: unescape any escape sequences in the path
Track line numbers carefully to extract exactly N lines
Create parent directories if they don't exist (mkdir -p equivalent)
Preserve file permissions and metadata from the original path if possible
Handle edge cases: empty files (N=0), files with no trailing newline

resoupify.description: Convert modified files back into a soup file
algorithm: 1. Identify all files you've modified or created during your work
a. Read the complete file content into memory
b. Count the total number of lines (split on newline, accounting for the final state)
c. Determine if the file ends with a newline character
d. Generate the soup block header: #SOUP "<path>" #SOUPED_LINES <count> #SOUP_TRAILING_NEWLINE <bool>
e. Append the header followed by the exact file content to the soup output
3. Write all concatenated soup blocks to a new file with a .soup extension
implementation_notes: Line count should match exactly what will be written
To count lines: split content by \n, but be careful with the final newline
- If content is "line1\nline2" (no trailing newline): line_count = 2, trailing=false
Escape special characters in file paths as needed for the soup format
Include all modified files, not just changed ones (in case deletions/renames matter)

malformed_headers.description: If a header doesn't match the expected format, treat it as a parsing error
malformed_headers.recovery: Report the line number and problematic content to the user

missing_metadata.description: If a soup block header is missing #SOUPED_LINES or #SOUP_TRAILING_NEWLINE
missing_metadata.recovery: Report what metadata is missing and which file path was partially parsed

line_count_mismatch.description: If fewer lines exist after a header than #SOUPED_LINES specifies
line_count_mismatch.recovery: Report the expected line count vs. actual lines found, and the affected file path

best_practices: Always validate soup block headers before consuming file content
Track your file modifications carefully so you can resoupify accurately
When desoupifying, create parent directories automatically
Preserve the exact line count when resoupifying—off-by-one errors will corrupt the soup file
Handle the trailing newline flag correctly; it's essential for exact file restoration
For files with no content, use #SOUPED_LINES 0 and #SOUP_TRAILING_NEWLINE false
If the user requests specific files to return, only resoupify those files
Document any files you create or modify that aren't in the original soup—add them to the resoupified output
